script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed two point four one percent down on the session. A broad chip selloff drove the damage, and memory names took the sharpest hits.

Micron and Sandisk led the carnage. The semiconductor index was rocked for the second consecutive session as memory-chip makers collapsed in unison — a signal that something structural may be shifting in the AI trade.

Wall Street snapped a two-day advance on Thursday as chip-related stocks slumped broadly. The Nasdaq led declines among major indexes, pressured by a wave of selling across the semiconductor complex. The catalyst: Taiwan Semiconductor's higher capital expenditure outlook, which rattled investors concerned about near-term margins and returns on AI infrastructure spending.

The top story Thursday belongs to Taiwan Semiconductor's capital expenditure announcement and its downstream effect across the portfolio. The company's elevated spending outlook raised immediate questions about return timelines on AI infrastructure investment — and the market answered swiftly. Chip stocks fell hard for the second straight session, with memory manufacturers absorbing the deepest losses. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index declined sharply, reflecting broad concern that the buildout phase of the AI cycle may be approaching a more scrutinized period. For this portfolio, the damage was concentrated and significant. Multiple positions with direct exposure to AI compute and memory infrastructure sold off in tandem, compressing the portfolio by more than two percent on a single session. Analysts are now watching whether this represents a temporary recalibration or the beginning of a more sustained rotation away from AI-adjacent chip names.

Now to the holdings rundown, starting with the largest positions.

Nvidia was down two point four zero percent on the session. The stock tracked the broader semiconductor selloff driven by Taiwan Semiconductor's capex concerns, which weighed on the entire AI infrastructure complex. Watch for any commentary from major data center customers that could stabilize sentiment.

Apple was up one point seven six percent on the session. Shares notched another record high on Thursday, with one analyst framing the company's platform strategy — its tightly integrated ecosystem of hardware and services — as a long-term competitive moat comparable to historic monopolies. Apple remains a notable outlier in a session dominated by red. Berkshire Hathaway's Warren Buffett also reaffirmed Apple as one of his preferred holdings, expressing confidence despite the announced departure of chief executive Tim Cook.

Alphabet was down four point four four percent on the session. The stock was cited directly in multiple market-wide summaries as a leading decliner in Thursday's AI-driven selloff. The company's exposure to AI infrastructure spending puts it in the crosshairs of any recalibration in that trade. Regulatory and competitive developments remain ongoing catalysts to monitor.

Micron was down five point six five percent on the session. The memory chip maker was among the hardest-hit names portfolio-wide, identified specifically in broad market coverage as a leader in the AI-related selloff. One report highlighted that a single company currently supplies fifty-six percent of the world's AI memory chips — framing the competitive stakes clearly. Any demand signal from hyperscalers could move Micron sharply in either direction.

Microsoft was up one point three eight percent on the session. Chief executive Satya Nadella publicly criticized restrictions placed by Anthropic on a product called Fable during a staff meeting, reportedly stating the limitations quote do not make sense. The comment signals Microsoft's intent to push boundaries in enterprise AI applications. The stock held gains despite sector-wide pressure, which speaks to its relative defensiveness within the technology space.

Amazon was down one point nine nine percent on the session. The company was named among analyst favorites within the so-called Magnificent Seven cohort, cited for what one report described as relatively low valuation compared to peers — the group's lowest relative valuations in a decade, according to that analysis. Consumer and cloud data remain the key near-term data points.

Advanced Micro Devices was down five point three three percent on the session. The stock moved in lockstep with the broader semiconductor complex, reflecting the same Taiwan Semiconductor-driven concerns weighing on Nvidia and Micron. There was no company-specific news to separate Advanced Micro Devices from the sector trend. Upcoming data center spending updates from major cloud providers will be pivotal.

Tesla was down zero point eight six percent on the session. The decline was modest relative to the broader portfolio damage, suggesting some insulation from the semiconductor-specific selling pressure. No company-specific catalysts emerged Thursday. Vehicle delivery data and any autonomous driving regulatory news remain the primary watchpoints.

Intel was down five point eight four percent on the session. The decline was the steepest among all primary holdings on a percentage basis. Intel carries ongoing structural challenges in both its foundry ambitions and competitive positioning, and Thursday's selloff compounded existing pressure. No company-specific news surfaced Thursday, but the magnitude of the move demands close monitoring into Friday.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Taiwan Semiconductor follow-up commentary or analyst notes responding to Thursday's capex disclosure — directly relevant to every chip name in this portfolio. Second, Sandisk's trajectory after a collapse of twelve point six three percent Thursday — a signal of stress in the memory supply chain that touches Micron. Third, watch for any Federal Reserve speaker appearances or scheduled economic data prints that could shift the rate environment affecting growth-oriented technology valuations.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch whether Friday brings stabilization in the semiconductor complex or a continuation of Thursday's broad-based AI trade pressure.
